summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim application – Risk – Where determination was made at an earlier hearing that child should spend no time with the father on an interim basis in circumstances where there are serious allegations of family violence and child has expressed fear of father – Where father now seeks that child live with him and spend time with mother based on allegations of risk arising from the mother’s mental health and substance dependency

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
The application before the Court is the father's Application in a Proceeding filed 20 February 2026 in which he seeks, in summary, that the parties’ child, X, born in 2014, be delivered up to him and that the child reside with him and spend no time with the mother pending a single expert psychiatric assessment of the mother.
The mother seeks the dismissal of that application and opposes an order for a single expert psychiatric assessment, proposing instead to rely on a report from her treating psychologist, who has been recently engaged following the retirement of her previous psychologist. She otherwise seeks various injunctions, the scope of which was refined by her during the course of the hearing, to adopt the position of the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) which is as follows:
That unless expressly invited by the child in writing, the father be restrained and an injunction is hereby granted restraining him from:
Attending at the child’s school, the location of any sport or extracurricular activity, or other location at which the child may be present from time to time; and/ or
Approaching within 50 metres of the child.
The mother's position with respect to the child's care arrangements is supported by the ICL. The ICL also supports the father's application for a single expert psychiatric assessment. It was agreed that if a single expert psychiatric assessment were to be ordered, it would be undertaken by Dr Marty Ewer in relation to both parties, and that the ICL would have the conduct of decision-making with respect to the documents to be provided to Dr Ewer.
The parties also agreed to an order that the mother undertake hair follicle testing for opiates, benzodiazepines and illicit substances, demonstrating six-month history, at her expense. The father sought a pharmacological analysis of the test results and it was agreed that if such an analysis were to be ordered it should be at the parties' joint expense.
Although both the mother and the ICL had initially sought orders relating to the production of documents by South Australia Police and the father in relation to criminal proceedings presently faced by the father, the Court was advised that those matters are listed for trial later in 2026. On that basis, it was agreed that the issue of production of documents pertaining to those proceedings would be revisited at a later date, noting that production made by the Police while the trial is pending may be limited and any disclosure provided at this point will likely soon be out of date.

BACKGROUND

This application comes before the Court in the context of competing parenting and financial proceedings between the parties, which have been on foot since January 2024 and were transferred to this Court on 15 December 2025. The proceedings are next listed for case management before Kari J on 30 March 2026.
Presently, the father and X are estranged. The father alleges that the estrangement has been caused or influenced by the mother. The mother asserts that the father’s conduct has caused X to fear him.
X lives with the mother and does not spend any time with the father. Although interim orders made on 18 July 2025 provided for a weekly phone call between X and the father, the evidence before the Court suggests that X has been unwilling to engage in these calls. X has not spent any time of significance with the father since January 2025, at which time the mother put an end to what had been a long-standing equal time arrangement, citing concerns for X’s safety.
The interim orders presently in force do not provide for X to time with the father. Those orders were made by a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) at a contested interim hearing following the mother’s review of an exercise of delegated judicial power by a Senior Judicial Registrar which had resulted in orders for professionally supervised time.
The Court has before it a family report prepared by Panel Family Consultant Ms C, dated 30 January 2026, as well as a child impact report prepared by Court Child Expert Ms B dated 14 July 2025. While those reports are untested, being independent expert evidence, I give their content significant weight at this interim stage.

RELEVANT LEGAL PRINCIPLES

To the extent that the orders sought are parenting orders,[1] section 60CA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) provides that the best interests of the child is the paramount consideration. To the extent that the application relates to injunctions, section 68B(1) of the Act empowers the Court to grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child.
I am guided in my determination by the objects in section 60B of the Act. It is well established in cases including the Full Court's decision in Goode & Goode,[2] that at an interim hearing where the evidence is untested, the Court must be circumspect with respect to findings of fact in relation to disputed issues and the process of inquiry is necessarily curtailed.
However, as emphasised by the Full Court in cases including Eaby & Speelman[3] and Salah & Salah (“Salah”),[4] the fact that evidence has not been tested does not mean that it can be ignored, and the likely impact on the child of each available course of action if the evidence were ultimately accepted must be taken into account. This is particularly the case in relation to evidence of risk and family violence.
As the Full Court held in Marvel & Marvel (No 2),[5] where risk is alleged in interim parenting proceedings, a conservative approach that is likely to avoid harm to a child is warranted. The Full Court has further held in cases, such as Deiter & Deiter[6] and Salah, that even where a determinative finding cannot be made in relation to allegations of violence at an interim stage, sufficient weight must be given to those allegations and an interim assessment must be made of the likelihood of violence and the severity of its likely impact.
The task I must undertake is to have regard to any uncontested facts and the state of the evidence before the Court and balance any risks apparent on the evidence in determining the arrangements that will best meet X's best interests pending a proper hearing.
As was submitted on behalf of the mother, in circumstances in which there has already been a judicial determination that X's best interests are served by spending no time with the father on an interim basis, the onus is on him to demonstrate some changed circumstance which would warrant revisiting that conclusion.
I have considered all of the evidence before the Court and the submissions made on behalf of each of the parties and the ICL. I do not propose to refer to or respond to each and every submission or refer to every aspect of the evidence in reaching a determination.
In determining X's best interests, I have regard to the factors enumerated in section 60CC of the Act, though as the Full Court held in Banks & Banks,[7] it is not necessary at an interim hearing to expressly consider each and every factor, and I will limit my consideration to the matters I have assessed as most relevant having regard to the issues pressed by the parties.
Factors set out in section 60CC(2) which are relevant are as follows:

(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child)

This is the most significant factor bearing on the applications presently before the Court.
The mother alleges that there are serious risks to X arising from family violence committed by the father, including family violence between the father and a subsequent partner of his, to which X was exposed.
The mother also alleges that there are serious risks to X associated with criminal conduct on the part of the father which are compounded by a failure on the part of the father to provide adequate disclosure in relation to his current criminal proceedings.
In relation to the risks alleged by the mother with respect to the father, the family report provides as follows:
Collateral information provided by the Police on [March 2025] noted that [Mr Church] had pending criminal charges (firearms, weapons and drug offences). Information noted that [Mr Church’s] criminal history included failing to cancel the registration of a transferred vehicle within 7 days (2021), driving with excessive blood alcohol (2010) and two convictions for driving unregistered (2007 and 2006). Police information noted allegations of family violence in [Mr Church’s] relationship between 2022 and 2024, including the issuance of two Intervention Orders (which were later revoked) as well as charges of aggravated assault and choking a person.
Both parents spoke about [Mr Church’s] current criminal matters. While [Ms Spencer] expressed concern about the risk this posed for [X] and this being a factor in her decision to withhold [X], [Mr Church] expressed his view [Ms Spencer] had been aware of his criminal charges for some time before she decided to withhold [X]. He said, “the day I was arrested, the Police rang and told her”. [Mr Church] was unsure why police contacted [Ms Spencer], but reported he had been in Melbourne and on his return, he handed himself in. [Ms Spencer] said she had been contacted by the Police the day before he was arrested, seeking his whereabouts, and then again to advise that [X] was with [Mr Church’s] cousin. [Mr Church] reported the drug charges had been withdrawn but the firearms offences were proceeding to a committal hearing in May 2026. [Mr Church] said he was surprised the charges were continuing and he did not think they should be proceeding. He described needing to obtain a DNA expert’s report as to how his DNA on the firearm could not be relied upon.
[Ms Spencer] expressed concern about [X’s] exposure to [Mr Church’s] behaviour in the context of family violence. She discussed the separation between her and [Mr Church], explaining that at the time they had recently moved into a new home and that, in the second week of living there, [Mr Church] “told us to get out”. She said it was over a “silly argument” and that, while driving, [Mr Church] was angry and had “smashed the inside of the car”. [Ms Spencer] said [X] had raised this event with her more recently and said, “do you remember that time dad was going to punch you... I was so scared”. She said [X] had been upset at the time and that [Mr Church] had been angered by [X] telling him to leave [Ms Spencer] alone. She said the next day [Mr Church] told her to go with [X] to her mother’s home and indicated he did not want her at their home. [Ms Spencer] said she had tried to reason with [Mr Church] to allow them to stay, but he did not allow it.
[Ms Spencer] alleged that [Mr Church] had grabbed her by the throat and pressed his chest against her. She said that, when angry, [Mr Church] had engaged in property damage, for example, throwing a toaster at the wall. [Ms Spencer] said she never reported such experiences, commenting that she was “young” and “scared” and did not know better at the time. Additionally, [Ms Spencer] said she was not someone who liked conflict and would try to avoid it.
[Ms Spencer] described elements of control in the relationship with [Mr Church]. For example, she said when he was angry, she would try to leave with [X] to go to her mother’s home to allow [Mr Church] time to “cool down”. She said [Mr Church] would not allow her to take [X] and gave the example of him pushing her up against a wall and “peeling” [X] out of her arms. She said, “other times I would get as far as the car and he would grab ([X]) and tell me to get the fuck out of here”.
[Ms Spencer] denied sexual abuse was a feature of the relationship, instead describing [Mr Church] engaged in degrading and emotionally abusive behaviour. For example, she said [Mr Church] had told her she was overweight and “repulsive”, and she was “lucky (he) did not cheat”.
Regarding allegations of stalking or monitoring type behaviours, [Ms Spencer] referred to [Mr Church] attending [X’s] school despite Orders for only telephone contact. She also referred to an incident when [X] was at [a soccer game] with his adult cousins, and he misplaced his phone. [Ms Spencer] said she called the phone, assuming whoever had it would answer when they saw ‘mum’ flash on the screen. She said it was not answered, but then she received contact from [Mr Church] to tell her he had the phone. [Ms Spencer] was unsure how [Mr Church] had managed to get the phone and felt [Mr Church] must have been watching [X].
[Mr Church] denied that family violence was a feature of the relationship he and [Ms Spencer] shared, but said he was aware she alleged otherwise. Regarding police information about family violence in a subsequent relationship (with [Ms D], [Mr Church] said, “it was a toxic relationship” and that “there were false allegations... they fell in her favour”. [Mr Church] then continued to explain that, as an alleged victim of domestic violence, “the Government” provided her with a place to stay, money, free legal advice and food. [Mr Church] alleged that on both occasions it was [Ms B] who was “out of control” and that he wanted her “removed”. [Mr Church] acknowledged [X] was present for the second incident but denied he had observed any of it or heard it as [X] was in the shower at the time. [Mr Church] said he was later contacted by NSW Police, who advised him that a report had been made by [Ms B] alleging family violence, but the Police advised him that on review of the information they considered he was “the victim in this”.
[Mr Church] raised the comments noted in the CIR about his behaviour. He disputed he had acted in a threatening manner, and considered he was “heightened to a standard level”. [Mr Church] said he spoke very much like his father, who he said was loud and aggressive and said “I’m my father’s son”. [Mr Church] said, “if I was heightened or whatever, nothing was to the (CIR) report writer, it was me expressing myself, it was not a threat to her” adding "it is just how I express myself”. [Mr Church] demonstrated by standing and hitting his flat open hand into the palm of his other hand and disputed he had used a fist as alleged in the CIR, adding he had apologised to the report writer but this information had not been included in the report. [Mr Church] said this had occurred after he was advised [X] would not participate in an observation session with him and while he had been expecting that, [Mr Church] said he did not like the CCE’s suggestion he engage in a men’s behaviour change program. [Mr Church] took this to imply he was the “problem”. He said it was for this reason he chose to see [Mr E] as a therapist. That said, [Mr Church] said he had recently engaged with [Mr F] at [G Mental Health Services] and had one session with him. [Mr Church] was confident about this intervention and planned to continue to participate with a view to a report being prepared for the Court. [Mr Church] said he had been engaged in the [O] program when he had been charged with family violence offences (in the past), stating “it was a great program”, noting he met people from “all walks of life” and it was “good to talk about stuff”. [Mr Church] said he was open to engage in any programs that may be of benefit to him.
[Mr Church] was asked if there were times him being “heightened” had created issues for him. [Mr Church] deflected the question to [Ms H], suggesting she was best placed to answer this question. [Ms H] described [Mr Church] as being “very animated” at times and said it took her a while to “get” [Mr Church]. [Ms H] said it took a while to understand that [Mr Church] was not “mad” and said he spoke loudly and was “expressive”. [Mr Church] said he had not experienced issues with his behaviour in the past, noting he had a good group of friends whom he had maintained since primary school. He said he had not been in trouble for fighting.
...
[Mr J] [the mother’s partner] expressed concern about the presence of family violence perpetrated by [Mr Church] and considered this violence to continued following separation. He described [Ms Spencer’s] and [X’s] fears of [Mr Church], including during the initial periods of withholding, and said they would not go anywhere without him due to their fear. [Mr J] reported that [X] had said that [Mr Church] had made comments about shooting [Ms Spencer]. [Mr J] disclosed his own experiences of family violence during childhood, stating that it was not until he and his brother were older that they were able to protect their mother. He reported engaging in support through the [K] program, which assisted him to better understanding family violence and his own experiences. Given this understanding, [Mr J] considered family violence to continue with [Mr Church] using the Court as a form of system’s abuse.

...
Information provided by the Department of Child Protection (DCP) on [March 2025] noted there was no open case for [X] but said there have been four notifications received since 2023. In February 2023, allegations were raised that [X] was present during an argument between [Mr Church] and his then-partner. In July 2023, concerns were received alleging an incident of family violence perpetrated by [Mr Church], including him grabbing his partner around the neck and dragging her through the home. In September 2024, it was alleged that [Mr Church] sent text messages to [X] asking him what to wear on a date, making comments about girls at his home and [X] "can have one”. Concerns were raised in February 2025 in relation to [Ms Spencer’s] mental health. Further information provided in July 2025 noted two further notifications. In March 2025, it was alleged that [Mr Church] perpetrated family violence, including verbal abuse of [Ms Spencer], property damage and coercive control. Information provided in March 2025 raised concerns about [X’s] exposure to inappropriate information, such as drug and gun raids, and messaging to [X] calling him a "bitch", "faggot" and "gay".

...
[X] reported experiences of being demeaned by [Mr Church], including being mocked and called derogatory names. He also reported exposure to adult conflict, including witnessing arguments between [Mr Church] and a former partner, as well as conflict between his parents when they lived together. [X] recalled an incident where he believed his father was about to hit his mother. [X] also reported being exposed to information and situations involving criminal behaviour while in [Mr Church’s] care, describing these experiences as frightening and confusing and contributing to his sense of being unsafe.

...
While the issue of family violence remains a matter for evidence, the information available suggests concern for [X] and his experiences of family violence perpetrated by [Mr Church]. Should the Court find validity to the allegations that [Mr Church] has perpetrated family violence, both in the relationship with [Ms Spencer] and in a subsequent relationship, the impact of this could include adverse effects on [X’s] emotional and psychological well-being, particularly where such behaviours may have compromised [X’s] sense of safety and security. While [Mr Church] disputed the allegations, it was noted he had recently commenced engagement with [Mr F] at [G Mental Health Services] with a view to addressing the allegations of family violence. This represents a positive step and may contribute to risk mitigation. The extent to which this intervention may result in insight, accountability, and behavioural change, however, remains to be established.

...
Also of concern was information about [X’s] potential exposure to risk-related behaviours associated with [Mr Church] and members of his paternal family. Information suggested that [X] may have been indirectly exposed to environments and circumstances connected with alleged criminal activity within the paternal family network, including situations facilitated by [Mr Church]. If the Court accepts the information, it would indicate that [X] has been present in settings associated with adult behaviours and matters that are inappropriate for a child of his developmental stage. Such exposure would be considered developmentally inappropriate for a child of [X’s] age. It was further noted that [X’s] reported experiences of safe and stable care within [Ms L’s] household may have heightened his awareness of differences between caregiving environments, potentially increasing the emotional impact of exposure to risk related circumstances.
The Department for Child Protection provided information to the Court pursuant to section 67ZBD of the Act, which included the following:
In [March 2025], the department received concerns pertaining to domestic violence perpetrated by the father, including severe verbal abuse of the mother, property damage and concerns regarding coercive control.

In [March 2025], the department received concerns that [X] had raised concerns regarding residing with the father as he did not attend school or pack food; that the father had spoken about nearby houses being raided for drugs and guns in front of [X]; that the father had sent [X] messages calling him “bitch”, “faggot” and “gay”.
Information provided by South Australia Police pursuant to section 67ZBD provides as follows:
NOT relating to other parties of this matter:

[...] – [...]/04/2024 – Domestic Abuse Offence – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance. Police spoke to [MR CHURCH] and the subject. Both alleging a heated disagreement, [MR CHURCH] alleging minor assault by cupboard being kicked closed on his arm causing minor injuries and slap or otherwise to head. The subject made counter allegations of rough conduct during heated argument and nil witnesses/ independent versions. Nil CCTV, Nil corroborative evidence beyond the very minor injuries. Both parties conceded that their relationship was over and went their separate ways, [MR CHURCH] back home and the subject went to the airport to return to their home interstate.

Outcome: Filed Pending Further Information

[...] – [...]/04/2024 – Domestic Abuse Offence – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance. The subject contacted police stating her partner, [MR CHURCH], closed the car door on her leg causing a cut, he then drove off in his vehicle. Police attended and observed the subject seated on the side of the road. [MR CHURCH] was leaving in his vehicle however stopped when he observed Police. Police obtained a version of events from either party. Counter allegations have been made and there is no evidence to support either parties version. [MR CHURCH] did not have any visible injuries. The subject had a superficial cut to her leg, she was conveyed to a hospital by Police. The subject was flying back home, interstate, tonight and was provided advice not to return to [MR CHURCH]’s address. Advice provided to both parties.

Outcome: Finalised

[Updated] [...] – [...]/01/2024 – Drug Incident – Police attended an address and conducted a search under the authority of a General Search Warrant. Large industrial shed on Northern end of property. Electricity was bypassed. Police located 2x very professional grow rooms concealed behind a wall at the rear of the shed. 12x large mature cannabis plants located and seized. Hundreds of ballasts, lights, globes, strippers etc also located. A vehicle located with registration documentation for [MR CHURCH]. On [...]/02/2024, police attended a second address and conducted a search under the authority of a General Search Warrant. Police located [firearms, rounds of various calibre ammunition and a taser]. Police also located an A4 colour photo of [MR CHURCH] and 2 other persons in full [gang colours]. Police arrested and charged [MR CHURCH] with [multiple offences].

Outcome: Next in Court [...]/08/2025 and [...]/08/2025

[...] – [...]/12/2023 – Domestic Abuse Offence – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance. Upon police arrival they were flagged down by [MR CHURCH] who had stated that his partner (subject) had allegedly assaulted him and stolen the keys to his vehicle. Police could see the subject in the background collecting her belongings and walking towards police. [MR CHURCH] stated that the subject had been staying with him over the Christmas period and this afternoon they have had a verbal disagreement where [MR CHURCH] has then told the subject that she cannot stay with him anymore as he is due to pick up his son and he does not want his son to be witness to their arguments. [MR CHURCH] stated that the subject has then asked him to take her to the airport to which [MR CHURCH] agreed. They have begun driving to the airport where they have continued to disagree with one another. [MR CHURCH] stated that the subject has then said that she no longer wanted to go to the airport and that she had cancelled her flight and that she wanted [MR CHURCH] to book her a hotel room to stay for the night. [MR CHURCH] stated that he told the subject that he was not going to pay for her to stay somewhere and he would rather she stay at his house and he would stay elsewhere for the night. [MR CHURCH] told police they have then arrived at his address and got out of his vehicle to retrieve her belongings from the boot and to place them inside the house. The subject has then walked to the driver side door and attempted to aggressively swing the door open, to cause damage to the vehicle. [MR CHURCH] has managed to stop the door from going past the stop point before damage occurred. [MR CHURCH] asked the subject why she has done this. He then stated she reached into the driver’s side of the vehicle and has retrieved the keys from the car and has begun walking off. [MR CHURCH] stated he confronted the subject and asked her to stop and to hand the keys back. [MR CHURCH] stated he grabbed the subject by the base of her jacket in an attempt to stop her from walking away. The subject has tried to pull away from [MR CHURCH], before she started kicking him in the legs (shoe mark visible to police on upper left thigh) and slapped him on the face. [MR CHURCH] stated he has then called police and continued to ask the subject for his car keys back, to which she refused. [MR CHURCH] stated to police that he wanted to press charges of assault against the subject, however then continued to ask police if he could sort the matter out privately with the subject without police involvement. Police spoke to the subject, who was vague on details. The subject stated [MR CHURCH] went to leave the address but would not let her inside the address. She stated she opened [MR CHURCH]’s car door and retrieved his car keys. She stated she did this so she could get back in the house. The subject stated [MR CHURCH], in an attempt to get his keys back, grabbed her jacket and wrist. She further stated he also pulled her hair. Police observed slight redness on the subject’s right wrist. The subject was happy for police to drive her to the hospital to have her wrist looked at. The subject booked a hotel for the night. She had not yet organised a return flight home and could not tell police when she would try to get a flight to return to home.

Outcome: Finalised

[...] – [...]/07/2023 – Domestic Abuse Offence – At about 1000hrs the victim was at [MR CHURCH]’s house. She wanted to leave and go home, but [MR CHURCH] wouldn’t let her. She got angry after [MR CHURCH] dropped her bags on the floor, and so she went up behind him and hit him to the head. [MR CHURCH] turned around and hit her to the left side of her head. She fell to the ground. She later discovered a cut behind her hear that bled onto her jumper. [MR CHURCH] grabbed her by the jumper, she got him to let go and she went into the bathroom. In the bathroom, [MR CHURCH] grabbed her from behind in headlock around her neck. She couldn’t yell out, and was struggling to breath. [MR CHURCH] dragged her to the front door and threw her out. The victim had a sore throat, and it was sore when she swallowed. Police arrested and charged [MR CHURCH] with 2 counts of Aggravated Assault and Choke a Person in a Domestic Setting. [MR CHURCH] was issued with an Intervention Order. On a later date, the victim requested to withdraw the charges.

Outcome: Withdrawn at Victim’s Request

[...] – [...]/07/2023 – Domestic Abuse Offence – At about 1000hrs [MR CHURCH] and the suspect were involved in a verbal altercation. [MR CHURCH] stated that he had been holding some bags belonging to the suspect during this altercation, in frustration he has dropped the bags on the ground and went to walk out of the room. Out of the corner of his eye he saw the suspect lunge towards him. He then felt a punch to the back of his head. [MR CHURCH] swung his body around to face the suspect, he then saw the suspect sat on the ground next to the wall, holding her head. [MR CHURCH] denied striking the suspect and was unsure how the suspect ended up on the ground. [X] was not witness to the assault – but was in the bathroom nearby to where it occurred. Counter allegations were made by the suspect ([...] refers).

Outcome: Filed Pending Further Information

[...] – [...]/04/2023 – Domestic Abuse No Offence – At about 1230hrs the subject called police for assistance after a separation with her partner, [MR CHURCH]. During the separation boxes of clothing were thrown onto the front driveway. Nil recent offences disclosed. Nil offences disclosed during this incident other than verbal insults.

[...] – [...]/02/2023 – Domestic Abuse Offence [High Risk] – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance. [MR CHURCH] had contacted police and stated that the victim was having a mental health episode and he wanted her removed. Police separated both parties and obtained version of events. Police spoke with the victim who disclosed an assault by being grabbed by wrist and thrown to ground. Nil injuries observed. Discussed with the victim her options – providing statement, pressing charges etc. The victim advised she would like to think about it. The victim stated she recorded [MR CHURCH] being aggressive with her phone, not the assault. The victim does not have any mental health issues and was not suicidal/intending on hurting herself. The victim appeared reasonable with police. The victim stated that she had "been hit" by [MR CHURCH] a few months ago but couldn't remember when. The victim stated to police that she was dragged out of the house by her hair but could not remember any other circumstances ([...] refers). The victim did not appear in fear of [MR CHURCH]. Police offered to drop her to a hotel or similar, however she wished to remain at the address in separate room. Police advised both persons to keep their distance and go to sleep in different rooms. [MR CHURCH] stated that there was an argument but didn't really want to go into it. He stated that victim went into his child's room and he told her to leave. [MR CHURCH] stated to police that the victim would say "something to get him into trouble." Nil independent witnesses. Nil immediate concerns for victim. At about 1540hrs on [...]/02/2023 police attended at the victim’s address as a result of her calling police after [MR CHURCH] locked her out of the house. The victim disclosed the events of the previous night where police attended, she now wanted to provide a statement in relation to those events as well as disclosing 4 prior incidents that each contained an assault dating back to June 2022. Police arrested and charged [MR CHURCH] with 5 counts of Aggravated Assault and Choke a Person in a Domestic Setting. [MR CHURCH] was issued with an Intervention Order. On a later date, the victim requested to withdraw the charges.

Outcome: Withdrawn at Victim’s Request

[...] – [...]/10/2022 – Domestic Abuse No Offence – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance. [MR CHURCH] stated that he had an argument with the subject about going out to dinner. He stated that he went to friend's house and the subject went out drinking. He stated that when he got home the subject wasn't there so he went to bed. He stated that that at some point he was woken up by the subject who had come home drunk. The argument continued and both parties threw papers and clothing around the house. [MR CHURCH] stated that the subject began to throw more and more papers, so he physically removed her from the house. The subject agreed with this version of events. Nil offences alleged.

Relating to both parties of this matter:

[New] [...] – [...]/03/2025 – Street Check – [MS SPENCER] attended a police station to report a breach of curfew bail by [MR CHURCH]. She stated that she was with their son when she saw him at the soccer. They did not communicate and [MR CHURCH] then sent a text message photo to their son of him in a corporate box. Her son then sent a text response saying 'leave me alone' and [MR CHURCH] did not reply. Police checked [MR CHURCH]'s bail and he does not have a curfew condition. Police asked how she obtained this information and she said her lawyers disclosed it to her. She went on further about a custody battle they were having currently and her frustration about [MR CHURCH] telling their son he's on bail and taking him to court with him.

[...] – [...]/01/2025 – Street Check – [MS SPENCER] wanted Police to be aware of a letter her lawyer would be sending [MR CHURCH] regarding custody of [X] and that [MS SPENCER] does not agree that [X] should spend further time with [MR CHURCH]. [MS SPENCER] stated that [MR CHURCH] has extensive criminal history and the letter will make him angry as he will not be able to see his son for a while. [MS SPENCER] was informed to call Police if [MR CHURCH] attends her home address looking for their son. Nil criminal offences disclosed.

[...] – [...]/12/2024 – Street Check – Police attended an address in relation to a domestic disturbance/check on welfare. Initial reports by [MS SPENCER] stating that she had been notified [MR CHURCH] was being verbally abusive towards [X] in the vicinity of the child’s school. Police spoke with [MR CHURCH] and [X] separately. [MR CHURCH] stated that [X] acted up and was verbally disciplined accordingly.

[MR CHURCH] stated that was his right to do so as his father and believed he did not step out of line. Nil physical. [X] stated the same versions of events – that he lied to his dad about school attendance and [MR CHURCH] told him off. Upon police arrival, [X] was happy in [MR CHURCH’s] presence, nil concerns for welfare, nil physical

injuries. [MS SPENCER] was notified that [X] was safe and happy in the presence of [MR CHURCH].

[...] – [...]/10/2024 – Street Check – [MR CHURCH] attended a police station in relation to concerns over the custody of [X]. [MR CHURCH] stated he had agreed (no court order) 50/50 custody of [X] with [MS SPENCER] . He stated that earlier this evening, he received a string of long text messages from [MS SPENCER] stating that she had made a report to Police about [MR CHURCH] 'taking [X] somewhere that he doesn't want to go'. and that Police had advised her to let [MR CHURCH] know not to pick up [X] from school tomorrow (as per their agreed handover). Nil reports on Police systems at time of [MR CHURCH] attending the station. [MR CHURCH] stated that he believed the text messages could have been malicious and she may not have actually contacted Police but he wanted a record made in case anything happened at school pickup. [MR CHURCH] also clarified that he had recently taken [X] to soccer trials at a club which he wasn't keen to play for and this is what [MS SPENCER] may be referring to in her texts.

[...] – [...]/09/2023 – Street Check – [MS SPENCER] attended a police station in relation to reporting a domestic violence incident. [MS SPENCER] stated that she and [MR CHURCH] began their relationship when they were 19yo, were married for ten years and divorced in 2021. [MS SPENCER] stated that during the majority of their relationship [MR CHURCH] would verbally abuse her and call her derogatory names. [MS SPENCER] stated that in early 2021 herself, [MR CHURCH] and her son, [X] were inside [MS SPENCER]'s vehicle when [MR CHURCH] got into a fit of rage regarding financial issues with his mother and father. [MS SPENCER] stated that [MR CHURCH] began driving at speed and hitting the wheel with his fist. They arrived home safe and [MR CHURCH]'s frustration continued into further verbal abuse. [MS SPENCER] stated that she endured years of domestic abuse from [MR CHURCH], however was hesitant to report anything to Police as she was in fear of repercussions at the time. [MS SPENCER] stated that she had a psychologist currently which assists herself and [X] to this day. [MS SPENCER] stated that she was aware [MR CHURCH] has a new partner and there had been domestic violence between the two of them. [MS SPENCER] wanted to find out further information regarding these matters in order to potentially assist with current fifty/fifty court order. Police unable to provide [MS SPENCER] with further information.
The father alleges that there are risks to X’s safety arising from mental health and substance dependency issues experienced by the mother. He says he learnt from subpoenaed documents that the mother has a history of diagnoses of bipolar disorder, anxiety, major depression and substance dependency and a history of multiple hospital admissions. He alleges that the mother has failed to provide adequate disclosure with respect to her mental health status. The father has also expressed a concern that the mother has negatively impacted X's mental health.
Counsel for the father tendered and took the Court through documents which unquestionably demonstrate that the mother has a lengthy psychiatric history and has long experienced struggles with misuse of prescription drugs in the form of opiates and benzodiazepines, though I accept the submission made on behalf of the mother that the status of the bipolar disorder diagnosis is somewhat ambiguous.
The mother deposes to having had a history of depression, which was known to the father, and which included a suicide attempt approximately one year into their relationship. She further deposes to having taken medication in accordance with prescriptions and to having developed a dependency leading to misuse of opiates during the parties’ relationship when X was very young for which she obtained assistance in 2023. She also deposes to having been diagnosed with bipolar disorder in 2006, which diagnosis was questioned when she undertook a psychiatric assessment in 2016. She otherwise deposes to having provided disclosure to the father in respect of her mental health.
The father's position is that the records now before the Court demonstrate that the mother's disclosure was less than fulsome. The mother, however, points to evidence filed by the father earlier in the proceedings, which supports her assertion that he did have substantial knowledge about her struggles with mental health and substance dependency prior to receipt of the documents upon which he now relies.
As to X's mental health, the mother deposes that he has been improving since he ceased spending time with the father and has engaged in ongoing psychological support, with his psychologist reporting no serious concerns. This is consistent with the reported discussion between the family report writer and X's psychologist, which is described at paragraph 54 of the family report. Although the mother asserts that X has threatened to take his own life, this was said to have been in the context of the prospect of being required to spend time with the father.
With respect to the issues of substance use and mental health, relevant paragraphs of the family report provide as follows:
[Mr Church] said he had left the relationship with [Ms Spencer] because “there was no connection” and she “lives on prescription drugs”. [Mr Church] said he did not have any further information due to limited contact with [Ms Spencer] over the last several years. He did report he had seen her “stoned” and this was the last time he had any interaction with her.
Mental Health
A report has been prepared by psychologist [Mr E] regarding [Mr Church] (dated 26 August 2025). It was reported that [Mr Church] had attended six appointments and that this was in line with the recommendations in the CIR. [Mr E] noted that [Mr Church] disputes any concerns about anger, reactivity, control towards others, inappropriate parent-child boundaries, or a lack of child focus. It was noted that [Mr Church’s] position was that [Ms Spencer] was intentionally discouraging [X’s] relationship with him. [Mr E] noted that there were no psychiatric issues present for [Mr Church], and his distress appeared to be primarily linked to the current parenting proceedings and the lack of his relationship with [X].
[Ms Spencer] said both she and [Mr Church] were prescribed medication for mental health. She noted she had not seen [Mr Church] for such a long time, she did not feel she could comment on his mental health. That said, [Ms Spencer] was of the view [Mr Church] required a psychiatric assessment noting his behaviour in the CIR.
[Mr Church] noted that [Ms Spencer] had informed him when they first met that she suffered from depression. He said he felt [Ms Spencer’s] mental health impacted her parenting as she was not making “any rational decisions”.
Regarding his own mental health, [Mr Church] said he was “drained” as a result of the current situation. He said he had been diagnosed with anxiety at 18 years of age and had been on medication since then. [Mr Church] said the medication addressed this for him, and he did not consider he was impacted by anxiety anymore.

...
Poor parental mental health was identified by both parents, with both parents acknowledging mental health issues for themselves, which they both currently described as stable and not impacting their parenting. Neither parent’s presentation at the assessment suggested concern about their current mental health, with both impressing as having awareness and ability to respond appropriately as required to address any mental health issues as they might arise. Unless the Court has information to suggest otherwise, parental mental health did not appear to be a significant risk factor in this matter.
The ICL submitted that whilst the materials produced under subpoena are suggestive of a long-standing history of mental health concerns for the mother, they also suggest that the mother cooperates and engages appropriately with her treating health professionals and has taken active steps to address her diagnoses including the opioid dependency.
The ICL further emphasises that despite the allegations with respect to the mother's mental health, X raised no concerns in the family report process that may be suggestive of the mother's mental health impacting on him in any way. Rather, X described the mother as positively meeting his needs. The ICL further submits that the available evidence demonstrates that the mother, despite her mental health diagnosis and history, continues to meet X's daily needs and provides him with a safe and stable environment. I accept that submission.
Notably, the father gives no evidence of any event or circumstance in which the child was placed at risk. Nor did he raise any such allegation with either the child impact report writer or the family report writer.
I note also that although the father emphasises that the mother's struggles have been in existence for a long period, he willingly agreed to an equal time arrangement for a period of at least three years following the parties’ separation, and thereafter sought and, based on the documents presently before the Court, continues to seek, court orders providing for X to live with the mother, after having observed her parenting directly over a period over the period of approximately seven years between X's birth and the parties’ separation. This gives rise to an inference that he did not consider that her struggles, whether he had full knowledge of them or not, had caused her to behave in a manner which placed the child at risk.
The issue of utmost importance to the Court is not whether the mother has mental health conditions or experiences substance abuse issues, but rather the extent of any impact of these matters on her parenting and any risk posed to the child. Furthermore, I accept the submission that the identification of any risk posed by the mother's mental health would not have the effect of overcoming the need to protect the child from the identified risks of harm in the father's care. The question of the safety of the child in the father's care on an interim basis has already been determined and the evidence adduced by the father does not overcome that conclusion.
The ICL further submitted, and I accept, that the mother's partner, who would appear on the available evidence to be a source of significant support for the child, represents a protective factor in the mother's household.
The evidence presently before the Court does not establish that the mother poses a risk to X's safety by reason of her mental health or substance dependence and nor does it support the need for his immediate removal from her care. Rather, the evidence suggests that there continues to be a need to protect X from the risks posed by the father and in that sense, the father has not demonstrated any significant change since the existing orders were made such as to warrant revisiting them.
The evidence does, however, suggest that evidence as to the mental health of both parties will be relevant to the determination of X's best interests at trial. I note that the child impact report expressly recommended that the father undertake a psychiatric assessment.
Both parties focused in their case outlines on the Full Court’s decision in L & T,[8] and the question of whether an order for a psychiatric assessment would need to be a condition of a parenting order in order to be within power. In my view, neither L & T[9] nor the more recent Full Court decision of Oberlin & Infeld,[10] both of which relate to orders for treatment, do not proscribe orders requiring attendance upon experts for the purpose of assessments to enable the determination of a child's best interests in the context of ongoing proceedings. Although the father's case outline made reference to a first instance decision from 2007 applying L & T to an order for a psychiatric assessment,[11] I note that in Lethbridge & Taylor[12] in 2020, an order for a psychiatric assessment was expressly upheld on appeal.
I accept the submission on behalf of the father and the ICL that the most appropriate means of obtaining evidence as to the mother's mental health is by way of an assessment by a single expert who can be jointly and appropriately instructed. Such a person should have relevant forensic expertise and should not have the concern of maintenance of a therapeutic relationship on an ongoing basis. This position is in accordance with the Rules.[13] I will therefore order that each of the parties undertake a psychiatric assessment with Dr Ewer. This does not, of course, prevent the mother from adducing evidence from a treating practitioner should she choose to do so.
I am also satisfied that the evidence warrants the agreed order for the mother to undertake a hair follicle test as sought by the father, and I will make that order. I accept the submission made on behalf of the father that the results may require analysis to be properly understood, though I do not accept that this will inevitably be the case. As a result, I will order that such an analysis be obtained at the joint expense of the parties in the event that it is requested by the father or the ICL upon production of the test result.

(b) any views expressed by the child

X has expressed a strong and consistent wish to remain living with the mother and not to spend time with the father.
The father alleges that this view has been influenced by the mother. Although this is an issue that will no doubt be explored at trial, at the present time, aside from the father's bare allegation, there is no evidence before the Court to support this view.
Relevant paragraphs of the family report provide as follows:
While at the assessment, [X] presented as a polite, articulate and mature young boy. He engaged respectfully throughout the interview and provided thoughtful and detailed responses to the questions put to him. [X] appeared to consider his answers carefully before responding. Although he displayed some mild nervousness during his interview with the report writer, evidenced by occasional hand fidgeting, he remained engaged and communicative. He conversed easily and sought clarification when needed by asking appropriate questions.
...
[Mr Church] did not identify any physical safety concerns for [X] but said he remained considerably concerned about [X’s] emotional and psychological well-being and was firm in his view that [Ms Spencer] was actively seeking to undermine his relationship with [X]. [Mr Church] said he found it difficult to understand that [Ms Spencer] was not correcting [X’s] negative behaviour towards him. For example, he said [X] had referred to him ([Mr Church]) as a “stalker and a weirdo” and said he would expect that [Ms Spencer] would correct such communication and would tell [X] it was not okay and to apologise, but said, “I just feel none of that is corrected”. [Mr Church] said, “I know for a fact she goes through his phone,” so he believed she was aware of the communication [X] had with him. [Mr Church] said he found it concerning that [Ms Spencer] allowed [X] to “act that way”. Further, he said this particular text messages had been sent after he had attended [X’s] end of school concert and sent [X] a picture of his artwork that he took, as well as a message telling him he loved him and was proud of him. [Mr Church] said this message had been sent by [X] when he was in the car with [Ms Spencer] and [Mr J], so he considered she was aware [X] had sent such a message.

...
... Since residing primarily with [Ms Spencer], [X] reported feeling safer, more settled and better supported. He noted improvements in his well-being, confidence, friendships and academic performance.
[X] said he does not wish to speak with [Mr Church] and experiences distress related to attempted contact. He described receiving messages in the past that he had perceived as inappropriate and expressed concern about the time of contact in relation to the court proceedings. [X] spoke about [Mr Church] attending his school which he said had caused him embarrassment and distress. He expressed a clear and strong wish to have no contact with [Mr Church], stating he wanted “nothing to do with him” and did not believe there was anything his father could do to change this. [X] expressed concern that contact with [Mr Church] would negatively impact his transition to high school and his emotional wellbeing.
[X] described feeling supported by [Ms Spencer], stating he felt she respected his wishes and prioritised his happiness and wellbeing. He expressed confidence that [Ms Spencer] would support contact if he requested it and he felt she wanted him to be happy.
[X] said he had experienced significant stress and anxiety in the past, including somatic symptoms such as stomach aches that affected his school attendance. [X] reported ongoing support including a psychologist, [Ms Spencer] and [Mr J].
When asked what he wanted to ensure was conveyed to the Court, [X] said that he does not wish to have any contact with [Mr Church] and asked whether it was possible for legal measures to be put in place to prevent contact. He emphasised his desire to feel safe and free from fear or pressure.

...
... Information provided by [X], was in line with the allegations raised by [Ms Spencer] and the collateral information. He reported feeling scared of his father when he was angry and described his father’s behaviours causing him to feel unsafe at times.

...
... Having regard to his age, and level of maturity, he appears to have a sound understanding of the current situation. If the Court accepts [X’s] account, his relationship with [Mr Church] appears to have been negatively affected by his experiences of [Mr Church’s] behaviour, including aggressive behaviour, the manner in which [Mr Church] relates to him and more recently, [X’s] perception that his wishes have not been respected by [Mr Church] in terms of their current contact. Overall, [X] appears burdened by the current situation and his reported fear in relation to [Mr Church] raises concern. Alternatively, should the court make Orders in line with [Mr Church’s] proposal requiring [X] to reside with him on an interim basis, this is likely to be experienced by [X] as distressing and potentially traumatic....
Having regard to the information obtained during the assessment, and in the context of the parents’ competing proposals, [X] has expressed a firm and consistent view that he does not wish to have contact with [Mr Church]. He has articulated reasons for this position, which appear coherent and thoughtfully considered and grounded in his reported experiences. Given his age and stage of development, he demonstrates a level of maturity that suggests significant weight should be given to his views. This position is further reflected in his current behaviour during telephone contact with [Mr Church], where he refuses to engage. In light of these factors, and balancing the risks associated with each proposal, it is considered that any contact between [X] and [Mr Church] should be subject to and guided by [X’s] wishes.
The views expressed by X to the family report writer were consistent with those expressed by him in the context of the earlier child impact report. The father submits that the child's view should be given limited weight in circumstances in which they were expressed in the context where he had not seen his father for a year (which I note was not the case at the time of the child impact report) and in circumstances where the father alleges malign influence on the part of the mother. I do not agree. Whilst those are issues that may well be explored at trial, a child’s expression of fear of a parent is a matter which warrants the utmost caution at an interim hearing and must be given significant weight.

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child's developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

The mother alleges that the father's parenting capacity and ability to provide for X's needs are significantly compromised. She asserts that this arises from his lack of insight into the impact of the conduct I have already discussed on X and from a willingness on the father's behalf to behave in an inappropriate and harmful manner towards him.
In this regard, the relevant paragraphs of the family report provide:
When asked about inappropriate text message communication with [X], [Mr Church] acknowledged that some of his messages were not appropriate. He said this was “addressed at the time” and that he had not considered it had impacted [X] until it was “pointed out” by [Ms Spencer]. [Mr Church] said “banter” between himself and [X] was a feature of their relationship and that there was a time when [X] contacted the zoo and alleged there was an orangutan on the loose, stating there was an "element of mucking around” in their relationship. [Mr Church] said he felt that [X] was “overwhelmed” by this issue because [Ms Spencer] “harps on it”. [Mr Church] acknowledged his messages had at times been inappropriate, but said [Ms Spencer] “made it a problem” for [X]. [Mr Church] said he had the opportunity to re-read some of the messages he sent to [X] and said he was surprised he had sent some of them. That said, he indicated it had been addressed and that he was aware it could send the wrong message to [X] about respecting women. [Mr Church] said he was focussed on ensuring [X] was taught good manners, such as opening the door or helping someone with their shopping.
...
[Ms Spencer] denied she had actively undermined [X’s] relationship with [Mr Church], commenting he had claimed “all sorts of alienation”. Had that been her intention, [Ms Spencer] said “I would have done that three years ago”. She described her attempts to co-parent with [Mr Church] in a manner whereby she tried to afford [X] a secure, predictable and stable home environment for [X] to counteract her concerns about his time with [Mr Church]. She noted that [X] never really spoke to her about his experiences until early 2025 and said [X] had been struggling to sleep due to a stomachache. She said she had asked him if there was anything on his mind, and [X] said he wanted to stay with her and not go to [Mr Church’s] home. [Ms Spencer] said that she would message [Mr Church] about it, but said [X] did not want her to, explaining he had asked [Mr Church] in the past about spending more time with [Ms Spencer] and he had said no. During this conversation, she said [X] told her [Mr Church] had taken him to Court with him and he was aware he had to check in at [City M] Police Station on a Friday. She further said [X] had informed her that the Police had found guns at [Mr Church’s] [Suburb N] Property and the police had also found drugs and had raided his [Suburb P] workshop. She said [X] had also told her there were times he wanted to come home early, but he was too scared because he thought [Mr Church] would get angry. She said [X] also reported that “dad talks to me like a 20 year old not a kid” and would make fun of him. [Ms Spencer] worried that [X] would feel guilt at the implications of his disclosures to her, so had reassured him she was aware of this information already, even though she had not been aware of it all.

...
[Ms Spencer] described manipulation by [Mr Church]. For example, she said she contacted the Police for a welfare check in the past, after a parent advised her that [Mr Church] was “losing it” at [X] and “screaming really angrily and loudly”. [Ms Spencer] said this was because [Mr Church] did not utilise the school apps, so did not have the information he needed at that time and instead placed responsibility on [X]. [Ms Spencer] said she arranged a welfare check and that when [X] returned, he asked her why she had called the police on him ([X]). [Ms Spencer] explained she had not called the police “on” him but was worried about him. She said it transpired that [Mr Church] had led [X] to believe [Ms Spencer] had called the Police on him ([X]) because [X] had done something wrong. [Ms Spencer] said [X] had also been concerned because when the Police attended, they spoke with him in his room and the PlayStation in his room had been stolen by [Mr Church] from a tenant who owed him rent.

...
Concerns were noted about [Mr Church’s] parenting capacity, both in terms of the allegations that he had engaged with [X] in a manner that was inappropriate, referring to the text messages noted in documents, as well as [X’s] exposure to criminal behaviours or activities. Regarding the text message communication, [Mr Church] did not dispute this information, instead demonstrating some reflective capacity, reporting that he was alarmed by his behaviour once it was identified, and he was open to being directed and educated that such contact is inappropriate. Notwithstanding this, it did not appear that [Mr Church] was able to recognise the inappropriateness of this behaviour until it was explicitly pointed out to him, which raises concern regarding his capacity to independently identify and maintain boundaries.
During the earlier child impact report appointments, the father was observed to become heightened quickly and is reported to have said in a “forceful, angry manner” that the child should be “forced” to stay with him.
The father's affidavit in support of the present application is completely silent as to the likely emotional impact on X of the significant disruption to his life and care arrangements proposed by the father, particularly in light of X's strongly expressed views and expressions of fear. That impact is likely to be profound and the orders sought by the father have the potential to be experienced by X as significantly traumatic.
In this regard, the family report says that should the Court make orders in line with the father’s proposal requiring X to reside with him on an interim basis, this is likely to be experienced by X as distressing and potentially traumatic.[14] Furthermore, the report identifies X’s perception that his wishes have not been respected by the father in terms of their current contact as being factor identified by X as having contributed to the deterioration in their relationship.
The father gives no indication whatsoever of having given any thought to these issues at all, much less any evidence of having developed strategies for how to manage them in a way that would minimise harm to X. He demonstrates no insight in this regard in his affidavit.
To the family report writer, the father predicted that X might say, “fuck you, Dad”, but would manage within a day or two. In my view this is a gross oversimplification of the complex issues in this case. This suggests a serious lack of insight and significant limitation to the father's capacity to provide for X's emotional needs.
The father alleges compromised parenting capacity on the mother's part in the context of what he characterises as “alienation.” In this regard, the family report provides that:
[Mr Church] is firm in his allegations that [Ms Spencer] is actively seeking to undermine and disrupt his relationship with [X], to the point that he considers [X] should be placed in his primary care in the interim. When at the assessment, [Ms Spencer] did not appear to present in a manner consistent with such an allegation. She appeared balanced and child- focused, reporting that her initial motivation was to seek reassurance regarding [X’s] safety prior to the resumption of spending time with [Mr Church]. She describes being guided by [X’s] expressed views and a desire to act in a manner that best supports his well- being. [Ms Spencer] reports that it would be easier for her if [X] were able to maintain a relationship with [Mr Church], as this would alleviate significant emotional and financial stressors for her. [X] did recall some positive experiences of [Mr Church], such as his joking behaviours. His acknowledgement of positive past experience does not exclude the possibility of undue influence; however, it provides some support for the view that [X’s] position is not solely a response to it. Taken together, these factors did not appear to support [Mr Church’s] assertions. That said, should the Court accept [Mr Church’s] allegations, this would raise concern for [X] as exposure to dynamics that undermine or distort a child’s relationship with a parent and can place the child in a loyalty conflict, compromise emotional security, and adversely affect the child’s psychological well-being.

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child's parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so

In this case while there could be a benefit to X of being able to have a relationship with the father, his strong resistance provides a significant impediment to this occurring. Furthermore, the evidence before the Court, albeit untested, casts some doubt on the extent to which this could be done safely.

(f) anything else that is relevant in particular circumstances of the child

The mother alleges that the father has attended events in X's life against his wishes despite there being no order for time between them. She alleges that this has caused X distress and fear.
The father does not deny having done so. His case outline describes this as “ordinary parental conduct” which might be the case in many circumstances, but in the context of this particular case, rather misses the point. The father has adduced no evidence about the important issue of the impact on X of this conduct in the circumstances of this particular case. The evidence as a whole suggests that the father's actions are likely undermining X's emotional welfare and sense of safety.
I accept the submission of the ICL that this is particularly significant in circumstances where sport has been identified as a source of emotional support for X and his continued engagement should not be undermined.
I am therefore satisfied that the injunctions sought by the mother and the ICL are appropriate for X’s welfare.
Having regard to all these considerations, I am otherwise satisfied that the existing arrangements continue to serve X's best interests.
